name: DEWALT 7-1/4-Inch Circular Saw with Electric Brake (DWE575SB)

specs.Bevel: 0°–57° bevel with positive stops at 22.5° and 45°
specs.Sight Line: Front bevel scale for easy bevel setting

whatWorks: The electric brake is the feature that separates the DWE575SB from budget circular saws and is the reason professionals specify it. When you release the trigger, the electric brake engages and stops the blade in under 2 seconds — versus 10+ seconds of coast-down on a non-braked saw. This is a safety feature and a productivity feature: you can set the saw down on a workpiece, step back, and reposition immediately rather than waiting for the blade to stop.

Fifteen amps at 5,200 RPM gives the DWE575SB the power to cut through the full range of dimensional lumber and sheet goods at full depth — 2-9/16 inches at 90 degrees covers 2× lumber in a single pass. Under load — ripping dense LVL or glulam beams, cutting multiple plies of plywood — the motor maintains speed where underpowered saws bog.

Bevel capacity to 57° with positive stops at 22.5° and 45° enables compound miter cuts and angled rips without reading the bevel scale at every common angle. The front-mounted bevel scale is easy to read while bending over the saw, which is a small but daily convenience on a framing site where settings change frequently.

At 8.8 lbs, the DWE575SB is on the lighter end for 15-amp circular saws. An entire day of carrying a saw from cut station to cut line adds up in arm fatigue, and the lower weight is felt by the end of a long day of framing cuts.
worthKnowing: The DWE575SB is a corded saw, not cordless. The 6-foot cord requires an extension for anything beyond a stationary cut station. On a framing site you'll typically run a 50-foot 12-gauge cord — at 15 amps, never use a 14-gauge extension, which will cause voltage drop and motor stress. The corded design also means the saw is never limited by battery charge mid-cut.

The blade is left-blade (motor on the right side when viewed from the operator position), which gives the operator a direct line of sight to the cut line when right-handed. Left-handed operators often find right-blade saws more comfortable. This is a personal preference but worth knowing before purchasing.

The included blade is a standard carbide-tipped construction blade suitable for framing lumber. For finish plywood cuts (cabinet sides, stair risers, finished flooring), a 40- or 60-tooth fine-tooth blade dramatically reduces tearout. Budget $25–$40 CAD for a quality finish blade alongside the saw.

Corded circular saws of this power class should be treated with the same safety discipline as angle grinders. Secure workpieces before cutting, never remove the blade guard, keep the blade guard clear of sawdust buildup, and ensure proper blade depth (1/4 inch below the cut material). Personal protective equipment — safety glasses, hearing protection — is mandatory.

commonComplaints.title: 6-ft cord needs an extension on a job site…
commonComplaints.body: The cord is too short for anything but a workbench setup. A 50-foot extension is the first accessory to buy. And make sure it's 12 gauge — this saw pulls 15 amps.

commonComplaints.title: Left-side blade takes adjustment for left-handers…
commonComplaints.body: I'm left-handed and the blade being on the left means the motor body blocks my view of the cut line. Not a design flaw — it's built for right-handers. Just something left-handed buyers should know.

commonComplaints.title: Stock blade is construction-grade only…
commonComplaints.body: The included blade is fine for framing but leaves tearout on finish plywood. Buy a 40-tooth or 60-tooth blade the same day for any work where the cut edge will be visible.

commonComplaints.title: Heavy for one-handed repositioning…
commonComplaints.body: At under 9 lbs it's not the heaviest saw, but a full day of framing with one hand guiding and one hand marking is tiring. Take breaks and use saw horses to reduce carrying.

verdict: The DWE575SB is the definitive corded 7-1/4-inch circular saw recommendation for framers, finish carpenters, and serious DIYers. The electric brake is genuinely important for daily use, 15 amps handles everything in the category, and 57° bevel with positive stops is as versatile as the form factor allows. Add a quality finish blade for non-framing work and a proper 12-gauge extension cord, and this saw covers the entire job site.

realBuyers.quote: I've framed houses with a lot of circular saws. The electric brake on this one genuinely surprised me the first time I used it. Two seconds to stop. You don't realize how much you miss it until you have it.
Used this to build a 400 sq ft deck — every cut for 6 weekends. Never overheated, never bogged, never missed. My most-used tool by far.
The 57-degree bevel lets me cut the compound angles on my stair stringers in one shot. Any saw that tops out at 45° would need two cuts. This is a time-saver on carpentry work.
Lighter than my old Skil and more powerful. Hauling it up a ladder all day used to destroy my shoulder. This one I can do all day.

metaDescription: DEWALT DWE575SB 7-1/4" circular saw review for Canada. 15 amp, electric brake, 57° bevel. Honest on cord length and left-blade orientation. ~$199 CAD.
introText: The DEWALT DWE575SB is a 15-amp corded 7-1/4-inch circular saw featuring an electric blade brake and 57° bevel capacity, designed for framing, sheet goods, and finish carpentry. Available on Amazon.ca (ASIN B00IXBNHPK) at around $199 CAD.
